references.title: Credit hours is not enough: Explaining undergraduate perceptions of course workload using LMS records

extracted.Features: [Average size of original posts, Numberr of Original Forum Posts, Number of OPriginal Top Forum Posts, Original Forum Posts per Student, TA/Instructoir Posts per Student, Average Forum Reply Time, %of students with replies, Average Size of Submission Comments, Number of Submission Comments per Student, %Submissions with Comments, Assignment Spread, Parallel Assignments, Course Assignments, Graded Course Assignments, Graded Course Assignments per Week, Most Graded Course Assigments per Week, Submission Time to Deadline, early Assignment Availability Ratio, early Assignment Availability Time, Dropout Ratio Q1, Q2, Q3, 4, Satisfied Prerequisites, Student Course STEM Match, Course credit Hours, Course GPA Spring 2021, GPA Standard Deviation for Course, Course was STEM, Number of Course Pre-Requisites, % Pass and Satisfactory among Non-Letter Grades, Course had No Letter Grades, No LMS Assignments, No LMS Assignments with Due, No LMS Assignments with Due + Unlock, No LMS Forum, No LMS Submissions, Multiple LMS Courses Concatenated]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data is collected from all sources and aggregated. Final dataset has each row refer to a learner-course pairing. 

Analysis starts with the creation of different linear models with different feature sets:

Individual Student Features from Enrollment, Course and Pre-requisite based features, Features based on prior student GPA, Feautres based on prior course GPA.

Dropout status of students was included as feature in a model.

Assignments were filtered in order make asure all assignments considered had, at least, one submission. Authors also created a set of control variables.

STEM status for all courses was encoded manually.

Variables whose Variance Inflation Factor was below 10 were excluded.
extracted.Implications:
Authors argue that ECTS are a bad predictor of the effort put by students into their studies. Instead, the authors argue that other indicators, such as the number of deliverables in the course should be used:

The more deliverables, the more the perceived effort the studuent needs to put into the course.

Time load is a determinant for elective course selection and should be treated accordingly.
extracted.Comments:
LMS features re collected at the course level.

3 Mixed Linear models were created:

- Time load, 
- Mental Effort,
- Psychological Stress

The authors show that the models containing these feature sets far surpass the expectations of effort students have about course load relative to ECTS.
extracted.Limitations:
Limited access to other likely predictors of course load such as lecture materials or Zoom calls.

Course load evaluations were done post-hoc, which may not be an accurate representation → peak-end rule.

Need to validate in additional cultural contexts. 

Sample was collected during Covid era - whuch may not be representative of workload in face-to-face contexts.

annotations.text: This study measures the degree to which student perception of the time they need to spend on a course actually maps to the credit hours expected for a specific course. Authors compare whether credit hours explain student effort (don’t seem to), as the number of deliverables student need to do actually is a much better predictor. Authors argue for the adoption of a new construct of course load than credit hours.
